summary: Fowler examines the proposition that UML could serve as a programming language, where detailed diagrams compile directly into executable code. He expresses skepticism about whether graphical programming is inherently more productive, noting his experience with failed graphical environments that were slower than writing code. He observes that even genuinely productive languages struggle for adoption due to factors beyond pure productivity. He warns that UML/MDA proponents often mistakenly believe they're achieving platform independence when they're actually committing to a different platform.
key_points: Graphical programming doesn't succeed merely by virtue of being graphical - usability and speed matter more
Drawing diagrams (like flowcharts) is often slower than writing equivalent code
Programming language adoption depends on many factors beyond raw productivity
UML and MDA constitute a platform commitment, not platform independence
money_quotes: I don't believe that graphical programming will succeed just because it's graphical.
Compare coding an algorithm to drawing a flow chart for it.
Most people I know don't program for a living in the language they consider to be the most productive.
Languages need a lot of things to come together for them to succeed.
Using the UML and MDA is a platform, and you are just as much committed to it as any other platform.
key_insight: The appeal of higher-level abstraction doesn't guarantee adoption or productivity gains—graphical programming must prove itself faster and more practical than writing code, not just more visual.
